title: Information Design in the Holdup Problem
abstract: We analyze a bilateral trade model where the buyer chooses the distribution of her valuation for the good. The seller, after observing the buyer’s distribution but not the realized valuation, makes a take-it-or-leave-it offer. If distributions are costless, the price and the payoffs of both the buyer and the seller are shown to be 1/e in the unique equilibrium outcome. The buyer’s equilibrium distribution generates a unit-elastic demand, and trade is ex post efficient. These properties are shown to be preserved even when different distributions are differentially costly as long as the cost is monotone in the dispersion of the distribution.
summary_en: A buyer is facing a seller who will make a take-it-or-leave-it offer. Can the buyer gain by choosing, upfront, how much uncertainty to have about her own value for the good? This theory paper shows that yes — the buyer strategically picks a distribution that creates a unit-elastic demand, ensuring the seller charges a low price and trade still occurs efficiently. In equilibrium both sides make a payoff of 1/e (~37%) of the gains from trade. The result generalises even when shaping one's information is costly.
policy_relevance: Relevant to procurement design and public-tender rules where the buyer (a ministry or state firm) can credibly commit to how it analyses information about price and quality before receiving offers. The paper's lesson is counter-intuitive: in some settings the buyer gains by not learning too precisely. Applies to Hungarian public-procurement policy (Közbeszerzési Hatóság): the prevailing rule of "gather maximum info before deciding" is not always welfare-maximising. Abstract; translation to a concrete procurement rule requires careful mechanism design.
